title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 12
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: You have been asked to write a report for your local council on how to improve the local high street, which has seen many shops close recently. Suggest ways to attract both businesses and shoppers back to the area.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Revitalising the High Street: A Strategic Report

Introduction
The aim of this report is to identify the causes of the recent decline in our local high street and to propose a series of measures to restore its economic and social vitality. This follows the closure of several key retail units over the past eighteen months.

Analysis of Current Decline
The primary factors contributing to the decline are twofold: the rise of online shopping and the high cost of business rates, which have driven small independent retailers away. Furthermore, shoppers have reported that the lack of affordable parking and the general 'unwelcoming' feel of the pedestrian areas discourage them from spending time in the centre.

Proposed Solutions
To reverse this trend, I recommend the following interventions:
- Diversification of Use: The council should encourage 'experience-based' businesses, such as workshops, galleries, and pop-up dining, rather than relying solely on traditional retail. This gives people a reason to visit that cannot be replicated online.
- Financial Incentives: Implementing a 'Business Rate Holiday' for the first six months for new independent shops would significantly lower the barrier to entry.
- Aesthetic and Infrastructure Upgrades: Investing in green spaces, modern street furniture, and free 'short-stay' parking zones would make the area more accessible and pleasant.

Conclusion
The high street must evolve from a place of mere transaction to a community hub. By combining financial support for businesses with an improved physical environment, we can ensure the high street remains the heart of our town.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You recently read an article in a magazine claiming that 'traditional museums are dead' and that all art should be digitised. Write a letter to the editor expressing your disagreement, explaining the value of seeing art and history in person.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the views presented in last week’s article, 'The Digital Tomb: Why Museums are Obsolete'. While I acknowledge the benefits of digitisation for archival purposes, the suggestion that digital screens could ever replace the physical experience of a museum is fundamentally flawed.

There is an inherent 'aura' to an original artefact or painting that a digital replica simply cannot convey. Seeing the actual brushstrokes of a Van Gogh or standing before an ancient Egyptian sarcophagus evokes a sense of scale, texture, and historical weight that is lost on a flat screen. These objects are tangible links to our collective past; they have a physical presence that commands a unique kind of attention and respect.

Furthermore, museums are not just storage facilities; they are curated social spaces. They provide a sanctuary for quiet reflection and a venue for communal learning. The act of physically walking through a gallery allows for a narrative experience that is far more immersive than clicking through an online gallery. 

In an increasingly virtual world, the role of the museum as a guardian of the 'real' is more important than ever. Instead of seeking to replace them, we should be using digital tools to enhance the physical visit. To suggest that museums are 'dead' is to ignore the profound human need for authentic, physical connection with our heritage.

Yours faithfully,

Simon Becket

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have been asked to write a review of a website or app that helps people learn a new craft or hobby (e.g., cooking, coding, photography). Describe how it works, its best features, and whether it’s suitable for complete beginners.
tasks.modelAnswer:
MasterClass: Learning from the Giants

If you have ever dreamt of learning filmmaking from Martin Scorsese or cooking from Gordon Ramsay, 'MasterClass' is the app that promises to make it happen. After spending three months exploring various courses on the platform, I can confidently say it is the gold standard for high-end hobbyist learning.

The app functions on a subscription basis, giving you access to hundreds of video lessons delivered by world-renowned experts. The production quality is cinematic; these are not your average shaky-camera tutorials. Each course is broken down into 10-20 minute segments, making it easy to fit learning into a busy schedule. 

The standout feature is the accompanying 'Class Workbook'. These downloadable PDFs provide detailed summaries, additional reading, and practical assignments that reinforce the video content. For a craft like cooking, having a well-organised, printable recipe guide is essential. 

Is it suitable for complete beginners? Mostly, yes. While some courses assume a basic level of knowledge, most instructors start with the fundamentals. For instance, the 'Introduction to Photography' course begins with simple camera settings before moving into complex lighting. However, the lack of a direct feedback loop—you cannot ask the instructor questions—means that absolute beginners might occasionally feel stuck on technical points.

In conclusion, MasterClass is an inspirational tool. While it may not replace a hands-on workshop, the sheer quality of the instruction makes it an incredible investment for anyone starting a new hobby. It turns your living room into a world-class studio.
